naturalMeaning: The default daytime greeting everywhere — walking into a shop without saying 'bonjour' first reads as rude in France.
Replaces 'bonjour' from roughly 6 p.m.; used both when arriving and (less often) when leaving.
Works for both hello and goodbye, but only with people you'd call 'tu'.

pronunciationNotes: Often compressed in speech to something like 'au r'voir' or even 'voir'.

naturalMeaning: The word for squeezing past someone in the métro or lightly bumping into them; 'excusez-moi' is for getting attention.
Agrees with the speaker: a woman says 'désolée' (same sound, different spelling).
In casual speech you'll constantly hear 'ouais' (yeah) instead — and 'si' replaces 'oui' when contradicting a negative question.

pronunciationNotes: Irregular: the first syllable is 'meu', not 'mon' — [mə.sjø]. Plural 'messieurs'.

naturalMeaning: 'Mademoiselle' for young women has largely fallen out of official and polite use — default to 'madame'.
Also the masculine indefinite article 'a/an'; feminine form is 'une'.

pronunciationNotes: The final -x is silent — but it surfaces as [z] in liaison: deux enfants [dø.z‿ɑ̃.fɑ̃].
Pronounced [sis] alone, [si] before a consonant (six personnes), [siz] in liaison (six ans).
The p is silent: [sɛt].

naturalMeaning: Also means 'brand new' as an adjective. In 'neuf heures' and 'neuf ans' the f is pronounced [v].

pronunciationNotes: Like 'six': [dis] alone, [di] before a consonant, [diz] in liaison.
The -gt is silent: [vɛ̃]. The t returns in vingt-et-un through vingt-neuf.

naturalMeaning: 80 and 90 are the famous oddballs on the way here: quatre-vingts (four twenties) and quatre-vingt-dix (four twenties and ten).
Never takes an -s: deux mille euros.

pronunciationNotes: The final -s is silent; singular and plural sound identical.

naturalMeaning: 'An' counts years (j'ai trente ans); 'année' emphasizes the duration or content of a year (bonne année !).

pronunciationNotes: The 'ui' is the gliding [ɥi] sound — round your lips for 'u' and slide to 'ee'.

examples.translation: Yesterday I ate at a restaurant.

examples.text: Je ne peux pas parler maintenant.

naturalMeaning: Context decides between 'always' and 'still': 'il est toujours là' can mean either.

examples.text: Je prends toujours un café le matin.
examples.translation: I always have a coffee in the morning.

naturalMeaning: No article for 'this Monday' (lundi, je travaille); 'le lundi' means 'on Mondays' habitually. Days are not capitalized in French.

examples.text: Le lundi, je commence à neuf heures.
examples.translation: On Mondays I start at nine.
Saturday night, shall we go out?

naturalMeaning: Many shops and most boulangeries' competitors close on Sunday afternoon — plan bread accordingly.

pronunciationNotes: The -ille is [ij], like 'fee-y': [fa.mij].

naturalMeaning: In everyday speech people usually say 'ma mère'; 'maman' is the affectionate 'mom'.
'Papa' is the affectionate 'dad'.

pronunciationNotes: Pronounced [fis] — the l is silent but the final s IS pronounced, unusually.

naturalMeaning: Means both 'daughter' (ma fille) and 'girl' (une fille) — context decides.
pronunciationNotes: -ille = [ij]: [fij], one syllable.
The œ ligature is the open 'eu' sound [œ], like the vowel in British 'fur'.

naturalMeaning: Same form for boys and girls; 'les enfants' is the everyday word for 'the kids'.

pronunciationNotes: The h is silent and triggers elision: l'homme, not 'le homme'.

naturalMeaning: 'Ma femme' means 'my wife'.
pronunciationNotes: Irregular: pronounced [fam], not [fɛm] — the e sounds like 'a'.

naturalMeaning: 'Amie' for a female friend — but beware: 'mon ami(e)' can sound romantic; French speakers usually say 'un ami' or 'un pote' (informal).

examples.text: C'est un ami de la fac.

naturalMeaning: Always plural: les gens sont sympas. For counting people, use 'personnes' (trois personnes).
In a restaurant, 'une carafe d'eau' gets you free tap water — no need to buy bottled.

examples.text: Une carafe d'eau, s'il vous plaît.

naturalMeaning: Ordering 'un café' gets you an espresso. Want milk? Ask for 'un café crème' (or 'un crème').

pronunciationNotes: Nasal [ɛ̃] — no 'n' sound at the end; the air goes through the nose.

examples.text: J'achète du pain tous les jours.

naturalMeaning: The daily loaf. Ask for 'une tradition' (baguette de tradition) for the artisanal one — most locals do.

examples.text: Une baguette, s'il vous plaît. — Bien cuite ?

naturalMeaning: A 'croissant au beurre' (all butter) is the good one; 'ordinaire' uses margarine.
Served after the main course and before (or instead of) dessert in a traditional French meal.

examples.text: Vous avez un plateau de fromages ?
examples.translation: Do you have a cheese platter?

examples.text: Un verre de vin rouge, s'il vous plaît.
examples.translation: A glass of red wine, please.

naturalMeaning: In a café, 'une pression' is a draft beer; 'un demi' is the standard 25 cl glass.

pronunciationNotes: Final -t silent: [lɛ].

naturalMeaning: For steak doneness: bleu (nearly raw), saignant (rare), à point (medium-rare), bien cuit (well done). French 'à point' is rarer than an American medium.
Also police slang ('cop') — 'les poulets' — which you should recognize but not use.

pronunciationNotes: Double s = [s]. With a single s it's 'poison' [pwa.zɔ̃] — poison. A minimal pair worth mastering before ordering dinner.

naturalMeaning: Masculine despite the -e ending: un légume.

pronunciationNotes: Final -t silent; 'ui' is the [ɥi] glide: [fʁɥi].

naturalMeaning: Often shortened to 'le petit-déj' in casual speech.
Lunch runs 12h-14h and is taken seriously; many shops still close for it outside big cities.

examples.translation: Shall we have lunch together tomorrow?

naturalMeaning: Dinner is late by anglophone standards — 19h30-21h is normal; restaurants may not serve before 19h.

examples.text: Tu veux dîner chez moi ce soir ?
examples.translation: Do you want to have dinner at my place tonight?

naturalMeaning: The waiter won't bring it until you ask — lingering is expected, not rude.
'À la maison' = at home. On a menu, 'fait maison' means homemade.

examples.text: Je rentre à la maison après le travail.

naturalMeaning: Casually shortened to 'appart': 'un petit appart à Paris'.

examples.text: J'ai réservé une chambre pour deux nuits.
examples.translation: I booked a room for two nights.

pronunciationNotes: The u is the tight French [y] — say 'ee' with rounded lips. 'Rue' vs 'roue' (wheel) is a classic pair.
Exception: -ille here is [il], not [ij] — 'ville' rhymes with 'peel', unlike 'fille'.

naturalMeaning: The anchor of every French neighborhood; most people have 'their' boulangerie and opinions about it.

examples.text: Il y a une bonne boulangerie au coin de la rue.
examples.translation: There's a good bakery on the corner.

naturalMeaning: The open-air weekly market — a social institution. 'Faire le marché' is going food shopping there.

examples.text: Le marché, c'est le dimanche matin.
examples.translation: The market is on Sunday mornings.

examples.text: Les magasins ferment à quelle heure ?
examples.translation: What time do the shops close?
Is the station far from here?

naturalMeaning: 'Métro, boulot, dodo' (metro, work, sleep) is the classic phrase for the Parisian grind.

examples.text: Je prends le métro tous les jours.
examples.translation: I take the metro every day.

naturalMeaning: Always plural: les toilettes. In a café you may need a door code from your receipt.

pronunciationNotes: Silent h with elision: l'hôtel. The circumflex marks a vanished s (hostel).

naturalMeaning: The informal everyday synonym is 'boulot'.

examples.text: Je vais au travail à huit heures.
examples.translation: I go to work at eight.

naturalMeaning: Literally 'silver'. Informal synonyms you'll hear constantly: 'du fric', 'des sous', 'de la thune'.
Prices are said with 'euro(s)' in the middle: 'trois euros cinquante' = €3.50.

examples.text: J'ai une chose à te dire.
examples.translation: I have something to tell you.

naturalMeaning: Wildly irregular: je suis, tu es, il est, nous sommes, vous êtes, ils sont. Also the auxiliary for motion/reflexive verbs in the past.
French uses avoir where English uses 'to be': j'ai faim (I'm hungry), j'ai 30 ans (I'm 30), j'ai froid (I'm cold).
Also the everyday future: 'je vais manger' = I'm going to eat. And the health check: 'ça va ?'
Everywhere: faire les courses (grocery shop), faire du sport, il fait froid (it's cold out), ça fait dix euros.

examples.text: Qu'est-ce que tu fais ce week-end ?
examples.translation: What are you doing this weekend?

naturalMeaning: 'Venir de + infinitive' means 'to have just done': je viens de manger = I just ate.

examples.translation: I come from the United States.

naturalMeaning: For knowing people or places, use 'connaître'. 'Je sais pas' — usually said 'chais pas' — may be the most common sentence in spoken French.

examples.translation: Do you know how to swim?

naturalMeaning: 'Je voudrais' (I would like) is the polite ordering form — 'je veux' sounds demanding at a counter.

examples.text: Je voudrais un café, s'il vous plaît.

translation: must / to have to; to owe

examples.translation: How much do I owe you?

naturalMeaning: The ordering verb: 'je prends un café'. Also for transport: prendre le métro.

examples.text: Je prends le train de neuf heures.
examples.translation: I'm taking the nine o'clock train.

naturalMeaning: 'Boire un verre' (have a drink) is the standard social invitation.

examples.translation: Do you want something to drink?

naturalMeaning: For residing somewhere; 'vivre' is living in the broad sense. Silent h: j'habite.
Careful: with people, 'je t'aime' = I love you, but 'je t'aime bien' = I like you (as a friend). The 'bien' weakens it.

examples.text: Tu peux me donner ton numéro ?
examples.translation: Can you give me your number?

naturalMeaning: 'Dis' / 'dites' opens a question ('say...'); 'ça te dit ?' means 'you up for it?'

examples.translation: How do you say 'cheese' in French?
I buy fruit at the market.

examples.text: J'étudie le français depuis six mois.
examples.translation: I've been studying French for six months.

naturalMeaning: Takes être in the past: je suis parti(e).

examples.text: Le train part à quelle heure ?
examples.translation: What time does the train leave?

naturalMeaning: 'J'arrive !' means 'coming!/be right there'. 'Je n'y arrive pas' = I can't manage it.

examples.translation: I'll be there in five minutes.

naturalMeaning: No preposition: chercher quelque chose (not 'chercher pour'). 'Aller chercher' = to go get/pick up.

examples.text: Je cherche la rue de Rivoli.
examples.translation: I'm looking for rue de Rivoli.

naturalMeaning: Reflexive: je m'appelle, tu t'appelles, il s'appelle.

examples.translation: My name is Claire. And you?

naturalMeaning: Feminine 'bonne'. Also the all-purpose conversation pivot: 'Bon, on y va ?' (Right, shall we go?)
Goes before the noun: un grand café. Feminine 'grande' — the d becomes audible.
Before the noun: un petit café — which as a phrase also just means 'a quick espresso'.

examples.translation: Shall we grab a quick coffee?

naturalMeaning: Feminine 'belle'; before a vowel the masculine becomes 'bel' (un bel homme). 'Il fait beau' = the weather's nice.
Feminine 'nouvelle'; 'nouvel' before a masculine vowel (un nouvel ami).
Feminine 'vieille'; 'vieil' before a masculine vowel (un vieil ami = an old friend).
'J'ai chaud' = I'm hot (never 'je suis chaud', which colloquially means 'I'm up for it'). 'Il fait chaud' = it's hot out.
Same pattern: j'ai froid (I'm cold), il fait froid (it's cold out).
After the noun for 'expensive' (un restaurant cher); before it for 'dear' (cher ami).
Informal amplified versions: crevé, mort (exhausted).

examples.text: Je suis content de te voir.

naturalMeaning: Goes before the noun: une jolie robe.
The accent distinguishes it from 'ou' (or) — same sound, different word.
Alone, 'Comment ?' is the polite 'what?/pardon?' — much better than 'Quoi ?'.
The answer starts with 'parce que' (because).
'C'est quoi ?' = what is it? As a bare reply, 'Quoi ?' is blunt — 'Comment ?' is politer. Also the untranslatable sentence-ending tic: 'C'est comme ça, quoi.'
Plain 'là' often just means 'here/there' in speech: 'je suis là' = I'm here.

examples.text: La boulangerie est là-bas, à droite.
examples.translation: The bakery is over there, on the right.

naturalMeaning: The workhorse of French conversation: ça va bien, très bien, c'est bien.
'Avoir mal à' = to hurt somewhere: j'ai mal à la tête (I have a headache). 'Pas mal' = not bad / quite good.
With nouns, always 'beaucoup de': beaucoup de monde (a lot of people).
Also 'in' for future time: dans dix minutes = in ten minutes.

examples.text: On se retrouve dans le café ?
examples.translation: Shall we meet inside the café?

naturalMeaning: Also an intensifier in speech: 'mais oui !' (of course!), 'mais non !' (no way!).

pronunciationNotes: Never liaised: 'et un café' has no [t] sound. Always just [e].

naturalMeaning: On forms, 'nom' = surname and 'prénom' = first name.

examples.text: Le français est une belle langue.

pronunciationNotes: Two syllables: [pe.i]. The -s is silent.

naturalMeaning: Feminine, so 'en France' (in/to France) — country genders drive the preposition: au Japon, aux États-Unis.

examples.text: Je vais en France cet été.
examples.translation: I'm going to France this summer.

naturalMeaning: Lowercase for the language and adjective (le français, un film français); capitalized only for the people (les Français).

examples.text: Il promène son chien tous les soirs.
examples.translation: He walks his dog every evening.

pronunciationNotes: Final -t silent: [ʃa].

examples.text: Je lis un livre en français.
examples.translation: I'm reading a book in French.

examples.text: Une table pour deux, s'il vous plaît.

naturalMeaning: 'Prendre un verre' = to have a drink; 'on va boire un verre ?' is the standard invitation out.

examples.text: On prend un verre après le boulot ?

naturalMeaning: Everyday speech prefers 'portable' for a mobile phone.

examples.text: Mon téléphone n'a plus de batterie.
examples.translation: My phone is out of battery.

naturalMeaning: Un sac à main = handbag; un sac à dos = backpack.

examples.text: Vous voulez un sac ? — Non merci.
examples.translation: Do you want a bag? — No thanks.

naturalMeaning: 'Faire du vélo' = to cycle; 'à vélo' = by bike.

examples.text: Je vais au travail à vélo.

naturalMeaning: The informal word is 'bagnole' or 'caisse'.

pronunciationNotes: The final s IS pronounced: [bys].

naturalMeaning: The high-speed train is 'le TGV'.

examples.text: Le train pour Marseille part de quelle voie ?
examples.translation: Which platform does the Marseille train leave from?

naturalMeaning: 'Billet' for trains/planes/shows; 'ticket' for métro/bus.

examples.text: Un billet pour Lyon, s'il vous plaît.
On va à la plage cet après-midi ?
examples.translation: Shall we go to the beach this afternoon?

examples.text: Il y a du soleil aujourd'hui.

naturalMeaning: The verb is 'pleuvoir': il pleut = it's raining.
One word, two meanings: 'je n'ai pas le temps' (no time) vs 'quel temps fait-il ?' (what's the weather?).

examples.text: Je n'ai pas le temps aujourd'hui.

naturalMeaning: 'Un chocolat chaud' — hot chocolate — is a standard café order, not just for kids.

examples.text: Un pain au chocolat, s'il vous plaît.

naturalMeaning: 'Pommes de terre' (earth apples) are potatoes; 'pommes frites' — fries.

examples.text: Un kilo de pommes, s'il vous plaît.

pronunciationNotes: Singular [œf], but the plural 'œufs' drops the f: [ø]. Un œuf, deux œufs [dø.z‿ø].

examples.text: Une baguette avec du beurre et de la confiture.
examples.translation: A baguette with butter and jam.

examples.text: Vous pouvez me passer le sel ?
examples.translation: Can you pass me the salt?

naturalMeaning: Context decides: carte bancaire (bank card), la carte (the menu), une carte de la ville (a city map).

examples.text: Un jus d'orange, s'il vous plaît.
Tu veux un morceau de gâteau ?
examples.translation: Do you want a piece of cake?

naturalMeaning: Usually ice cream in a food context; 'des glaçons' are ice cubes — ask, because drinks come without.

examples.text: Une glace à la vanille, s'il vous plaît.

naturalMeaning: 'Serrer la main' = to shake hands, the default greeting between men and in professional settings.

examples.text: On se serre la main ou on se fait la bise ?
examples.translation: Do we shake hands or kiss cheeks?

naturalMeaning: Irregular plural of 'œil' (eye): un œil, des yeux.
Also 'very rare' for steak, a bruise, and a rookie — French loves this word.
Feminine 'blanche'. Un (vin) blanc = a white wine.
Feminine 'verte' — the t becomes audible. 'Vert', 'verre' (glass), 'vers' (toward) and 'ver' (worm) all sound identical: [vɛʁ].
'Encore un peu ?' = a bit more? 'Il est encore là' = he's still here. 'Encore !' = again!

examples.text: Encore un café, s'il vous plaît.

naturalMeaning: Also the tip-of-the-tongue tic: 'C'est quoi ton nom, déjà ?' (What's your name again?)
In casual speech also a plain intensifier: 'c'est trop bien !' (it's so good!).

examples.translation: Shall we have dinner together tonight?

naturalMeaning: Untranslatable and essential: chez moi (my place), chez le médecin (at the doctor's), chez Paul (Paul's).

examples.translation: Shall we meet at my place?

naturalMeaning: Yes, French borrowed it — with a hyphen. 'Bon week-end !' is the Friday goodbye.

examples.text: Qu'est-ce que tu fais ce week-end ?
examples.translation: What are you doing this weekend?
What music do you listen to?

naturalMeaning: Going to the movies is 'aller au ciné(ma)'.

examples.text: On va voir un film ce soir ?
examples.translation: Shall we see a movie tonight?

naturalMeaning: 'Faire du sport' = to exercise / play sports.

examples.translation: Do you play sports / work out?

naturalMeaning: Short for 'football' — the everyday word. 'Les Bleus' is the national team.

examples.text: On regarde le foot ce soir ?
examples.translation: Shall we watch the game tonight?

examples.text: Vous pouvez nous prendre en photo ?
examples.translation: Can you take a photo of us?

naturalMeaning: Feminine 'gentille' [ʒɑ̃.tij]. 'C'est gentil !' is the standard response to a favor.

examples.text: C'est très gentil de votre part.

naturalMeaning: 'C'est vrai ?' = really? 'C'est pas vrai !' = no way!

examples.text: Une autre bière, s'il vous plaît.

naturalMeaning: Everywhere: c'est tout (that's all), tout le monde (everyone), tout de suite (right away).
'Tout le monde' = everyone; 'il y a du monde' = it's crowded.

examples.text: Il y a trop de monde ici.
